result.question:
How to minimize wall-clock time for (A) on-policy self-distillation DATA CAPTURE and (B) draft-head TRAINING, when adapting a DeepSeek MTP/DSpark speculative-decoding draft head to a REAP-pruned, NVFP4-quantized ~180B MoE target on a SINGLE 128GB unified-memory device (Jetson Thor, ~19 tok/s single-stream decode, the 96GiB weights are single-tenant so nothing else can hold the model concurrently).

The training is OFFLINE: the modified (REAP+NVFP4) target must generate the training responses on-policy (self-distillation), and we cache what the head needs; then we train only the ~3%-trainable head (which is itself an MLA + 256-expert top-6 MoE MTP layer) for 1-3 warm-started epochs. Our pre-flight estimate is capture-dominated (~16-70 hrs capture + a few-20 hrs train). We want to cut both.

Research and synthesize, with citations and concrete numbers where they exist:

CAPTURE (the bottleneck):
1. Throughput-oriented generation on a single device: how much does continuous/dynamic batching raise aggregate tok/s for a large MoE vs single-stream latency-optimized decode? Concrete batch-size vs throughput curves for MoE inference (vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM). Memory-bandwidth vs compute crossover for MoE decode.
2. LOSSLESS speculative decoding to accelerate the CAPTURE generation itself: since accepted tokens are exactly the target's distribution, can we use the unfine-tuned draft head (or any draft) to speed the target's own generation while capturing training data for that same head — i.e. bootstrap? Any precedent for spec-decoding the data-generation pass. Does using MTP/multi-token prediction during capture preserve the exact sampled distribution?
3. What MUST be captured for MTP/EAGLE-3-style TOKEN cross-entropy + logit-KD training: is it tokens only, or tokens + top-k target logits (what k is enough — 8/16/32?), or also last hidden states? EAGLE-3 abandoned feature/hidden-state regression — does that mean hidden-state capture can be SKIPPED, cutting capture compute/storage/IO? Storage footprint per token for each option.
4. Is FULL on-policy generation necessary, or does a HYBRID (existing instruction corpus + target-REGENERATED responses, or partial/prefix regeneration, temperature-0.8 regeneration a la SpecForge) capture most of the distribution-alignment benefit at a fraction of the generation cost? Disclosed ablations on self-generated vs regenerated vs static data.
5. Minimum data to converge a WARM-STARTED draft head: fewest tokens/examples that still recover acceptance (so we generate less). Any token-count scaling curves.

TRAINING:
6. Cache-once-train-many: precompute/cache target outputs once, then train the head over many epochs without re-running the 96GiB target. Storage vs recompute tradeoff; how EAGLE/SpecForge/FastMTP structure the offline cache.
7. Fastest head-training recipe: disclosed single-node wall-times (FastMTP <1 day on one H20), batch size, epochs, LoRA-on-head vs full-head fine-tune (does LoRA cut time without hurting acceptance?), efficient training of a small MoE head (only active experts get gradients).
8. Any 2025-2026 tooling specifically for accelerating speculative-decoding / draft-head data pipelines and training on a single or edge device (SpecForge offline mode, vLLM 'speculators', NeMo EAGLE recipes, on-device training).

Prioritize concrete, implementable levers with real numbers over generalities. Deliverable feeds a plan to cut a ~1-4 day capture+train run to as short as possible on one Jetson Thor.
result.summary: To cut the capture+train run on one Jetson Thor, the highest-leverage move is to abandon single-stream (latency-optimized) generation for throughput-oriented batched decode: batching an MoE on one device raises aggregate tok/s by roughly an order of magnitude (30-40x for a 236B MoE via module-based batching, ~3-4x from batch 16->128 for dense LLMs, and >90x vs single-stream in the extreme), because larger per-expert batches raise arithmetic intensity and move MoE decode from bandwidth-bound toward compute-bound — but this benefit saturates earlier for MoE than for dense models since activated experts grow with batch size. A second, orthogonal and provably lossless lever is to spec-decode the capture pass itself using the (even un-fine-tuned) draft head or any drafter: rejection-sampling verification preserves the exact target distribution, so the captured data stays on-policy while rollout wall-clock drops 1.5-1.8x (measured, 8B) up to ~50% (RL rollouts). For what to cache, EAGLE-3 dropped the feature-regression LOSS (train tokens-only with multi-step simulation) but still CONSUMES concatenated low/mid/high target hidden states as draft input, so hidden-state capture cannot be fully skipped; SpecForge's offline mode precomputes these once (enabling cache-once-train-many on a single GPU) at a large disk cost (~12TB for UltraChat+ShareGPT). Fully on-policy self-distillation is the reference recipe (FastMTP, Draft-OPD), and it is cheap to TRAIN: a ~210M-param MTP head (<3% of a 7B model) trained to convergence in <1 day on one H20 over 3 epochs / 389K self-distilled samples at global batch 64 — confirming the pre-flight intuition that capture, not training, is the bottleneck to attack.

findings.claim: Switching capture from single-stream latency decode to throughput-oriented batched/module-based decode is the single largest wall-clock lever, raising aggregate MoE tok/s by roughly an order of magnitude to 30-40x on one device.
findings.evidence: MoE-Gen module-based batching (accumulate tokens in host memory, launch large per-module batches): DeepSeek-V2-236B decode 31 tok/s vs vLLM 0.8 and DeepSpeed 1 tok/s (~30-40x); Mixtral-8x7B 469 vs 33 tok/s (FlexGen); 8-31x over model-based batching baselines. Bench360 (dense) shows ~3-4x from batch 16->128 (vLLM A30 318.6->964.4 TPS) and single-stream TPOT ~0.09-0.095 s/tok (~10-11 tok/s) vs 964 TPS aggregate => >90x. MoE-Lightning explicitly frames batched throughput inference as the tool for offline synthetic-data-generation where higher throughput = lower total completion time.

findings.claim: The batching win for MoE saturates earlier than for a dense model, so the achievable capture speedup on Thor is bounded by how many tokens per expert you can accumulate, not unlimited.
findings.evidence: MoE effective compute converges from small-active-param behavior (4B-like) toward full dense equivalent (32B-like) as batch grows because activated parameters increase with batch. Arithmetic intensity I ~= n(Nk/Ne) rises with batch n and activated-expert fraction; for a 256-expert top-6 head, per-expert token count (not raw global batch) governs decode, so much larger batches are needed to reach the compute-bound crossover. MoE-Gen decode reached 75 tokens/expert at 41% utilization vs baseline 0.3-0.4 tokens/expert at 0.1%.

findings.claim: Spec-decoding the capture pass itself is provably lossless (rejection sampling preserves the exact target distribution), so an un-fine-tuned MTP/EAGLE draft head can bootstrap-accelerate generation of its own on-policy training data.
findings.evidence: NVIDIA Nemotron: EAGLE-3 spec-decoding accelerates on-policy rollout GENERATION 1.5-1.8x measured on 8B (RL-Zero 100->56.6s/step=1.8x; RL-Think 133.6->87.0s=1.5x), with rejection procedure guaranteeing rollouts still follow the verifier/target policy 'without changing the sampling distribution.' DAS (MLSys 2026 oral) reduces rollout time up to 50% with identical training curves. Losslessness rests on the Leviathan/Chen 2023 rejection-sampling theorem, so accepted tokens are exactly on-policy up to numeric precision.

findings.claim: CRITICAL CAVEAT for bootstrapping: losslessness holds ONLY for exact rejection sampling, not for relaxed/typical MTP acceptance (e.g. Medusa typical-acceptance), and target-assisted generation can eliminate the pure on-policy signal.
findings.evidence: Relaxed acceptance variants (typical/top-m/entropy thresholds) are explicitly NOT distribution-preserving; capture must use exact rejection sampling to stay exactly on-policy. Draft-OPD notes target-assisted generation makes collected sequences follow the target distribution and 'thus eliminates the on-policy signal' for the strongest OPD variant — i.e. spec-decoded capture yields correct target-distribution tokens but the very strongest signal comes from replaying draft-induced error states, a subtlety beyond plain online generation.

findings.claim: EAGLE-3 abandoned the hidden-state/feature REGRESSION loss (trains tokens-only cross-entropy with multi-step generation simulation), but the draft head still CONSUMES concatenated low/mid/high target hidden states as input — so hidden-state capture cannot be fully skipped, only its regression target can.
findings.evidence: EAGLE-3 'removes the feature prediction constraint and directly predict tokens while simulating multi-step generation during training,' yet records low/middle/high feature sequences (l,m,h), concatenates to a 3k-dim vector and projects through an FC layer to k-dim as the draft input g. So capture must still store the fused l/m/h target features (plus tokens); dropping the loss cuts a training constraint, not the capture footprint.

findings.claim: Cache-once-train-many is directly supported: SpecForge offline mode precomputes target hidden states once and reuses them across epochs so training runs on a single GPU without the 96GiB target loaded — at a large disk cost (~12TB for UltraChat+ShareGPT).
findings.evidence: SpecForge offers offline (precompute hidden states once, high disk ~12TB, no extra target GPUs at train time) vs online (generate on the fly, low disk, needs more GPUs for large targets). For a single Thor this argues: run capture once (the throughput-batched + spec-decoded pass), persist tokens + fused hidden states, then train the head many epochs without re-running the target. Storage is the tradeoff to manage on a 128GB unified device (stream cache from NVMe).

findings.claim: Training the head is cheap and NOT the bottleneck: a ~210M-param MTP head (<3% of a 7B model) trained to convergence in <1 day on one H20 server, 3 epochs over 389.4K fully self-distilled samples at global batch 64.
findings.evidence: FastMTP: 210.8M params (<3% of 7833.4M), 3 epochs / 389.4K samples / global batch 64, entire training <1 day on a single H20 server. SpecForge draft heads for Llama-4 Scout/Maverick trained on 320K ShareGPT+UltraChat samples yielding 2.0-2.18x MT-Bench speedups. These confirm training is hours-scale; wall-clock effort should be spent on capture. (Note H20 is far larger than Thor, so absolute train time will be longer, but relative cheapness vs capture holds.)

findings.claim: Fully on-policy self-distillation is the disclosed reference recipe (all responses generated by the target itself), and it matters because continued offline SFT on static reference answers plateaus due to an offline-to-inference distribution mismatch.
findings.evidence: FastMTP generates all training responses with the main model (temp 0.6, top-k 20, top-p 0.95, max 4096 tok, via SGLang). Draft-OPD: 'After an initial SFT warm-up, continued offline SFT quickly plateaus... offline-to-inference mismatch,' motivating online target-generated responses. Note: the adversarial pass REFUTED the specific claim that on-policy measurably beats regenerated rollouts (4.63x->4.29x was within noise, vote 1-2), so a HYBRID (regenerated/temp-0.8) may recover most benefit at lower cost — but no strong disclosed ablation confirmed the exact fraction.
findings.vote: 3-0 on claims 17,18; related regeneration-vs-onpolicy claim REFUTED 1-2

result.caveats: Time-sensitivity: nearly all concrete numbers are 2025-2026 self-reported systems-paper benchmarks (MoE-Gen, Bench360, MoE-Lightning, FastMTP, SpecForge, DAS, Nemotron) with the usual vendor/baseline-selection bias. HARDWARE TRANSFER IS THE BIGGEST GAP: the 30-40x MoE-Gen and >90x Bench360 gains come from memory-constrained OFFLOADING regimes (24GB A5000 + 512GB host, or datacenter A10/A30) where baselines are slow precisely because the model does not fit in GPU memory. On a Jetson Thor where the ~96GiB weights already fit in 128GB unified memory, the single-stream baseline is NOT offload-crippled, so the realistic batching speedup is closer to the dense ~3-4x (batch 16->128) roofline figure than to 30-90x, further capped by MoE early saturation (activated experts grow with batch) and by only ~32GB of headroom left for KV-cache/activations limiting max batch. Bench360 numbers are DENSE models, not a 180B MoE. FastMTP's '<1 day on a single H20' is a much larger/faster GPU than Thor and 'single H20 server' may be multi-GPU, so on-device train time will be longer (though still likely hours). Storage: SpecForge offline cache is ~12TB for two datasets — for a subset sized to a warm-started head this shrinks, but hidden-state capture (EAGLE-3 still needs fused l/m/h features) dominates per-token footprint; a tokens+top-k-logits-only cache (if the head uses logit-KD rather than feature input) would be far smaller, but the exact minimum k (8/16/32) and whether hidden states can be dropped for THIS DSpark/MTP head was not resolved by any surviving claim. The on-policy-vs-regenerated advantage was REFUTED as within-noise, so the cost-saving hybrid path is plausible but not quantified.
openQuestions: What is the realistic batched-decode speedup for a REAP-pruned NVFP4 180B MoE that already FITS in 128GB unified memory (no offloading), and what max batch size does the ~32GB non-weight headroom allow before KV-cache/activation pressure caps it? None of the surviving benchmarks measure the fits-in-memory regime on Thor-class hardware.

For this specific DSpark/DeepSeek-MTP head, is hidden-state capture actually required, or can capture be reduced to tokens + top-k target logits only (and is k=8/16/32 sufficient)? EAGLE-3 still consumes fused l/m/h features, but MTP/logit-KD heads may not — this directly determines capture compute/storage/IO.

Does a HYBRID corpus (static instruction data + partial/temperature-0.8 target regeneration) recover most of the acceptance benefit at a fraction of full on-policy generation cost? The on-policy-beats-regenerated claim was refuted as within-noise, but no disclosed ablation quantifies the hybrid fraction for a warm-started head.

What is the minimum token/example count to converge a WARM-STARTED draft head and recover acceptance (so less data must be generated)? No surviving claim provided a token-count scaling curve; FastMTP used 389K samples but did not report a lower bound.

refuted.claim: MoE decode at the moderate batch sizes imposed by latency SLOs is memory-bandwidth-bound, with latency scaling directly with the number of distinct experts that must be loaded from HBM — so batching quickly negates MoE sparsity because a small batch already forces loading nearly all experts.

Batching-oriented MoE serving (MoE-Lens) achieves on average 4.6x and up to 25.5x higher throughput than the prior state-of-the-art throughput system MoE-Lightning, demonstrating large aggregate tok/s gains from throughput-optimized (batched) MoE decode over latency-oriented approaches.

Throughput-oriented batched MoE inference with weight offloading achieves up to 10.3x higher aggregate token throughput than state-of-the-art offloading LLM systems on a single memory-constrained GPU (Mixtral 8x7B on one 16GB T4), demonstrating that continuous/dynamic batching dramatically raises aggregate tok/s for large MoE decode versus latency-optimized single-stream serving.

On-policy distillation beats target-assisted regenerated rollouts for draft-head acceptance: naive target-assisted rollout drops speedup from 4.63x to 4.29x (7.3% relative), so full on-policy self-distillation is measurably better than regenerated/static supervision.
